title: Frobenius distribution for pairs of elliptic curves and exceptional isogenies
speaker: François Charles
tags: Workshop on Chow Groups Motives and Derived Categories

proof_stages.label: Noether-Lefschetz locus and complex K3 prototype
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines the Noether-Lefschetz locus as the set of fibers with extra divisor classes and recalls Green's proof that, for a non-isotrivial family of K3 surfaces over a disk, variation of Hodge structure plus local Torelli makes rational Hodge classes appear densely on nearby fibers.

proof_stages.label: Equal-characteristic elliptic-surface motivation
proof_stages.summary: For a curve over a finite field and a product of two elliptic curves, the Noether-Lefschetz condition becomes isogeny of the two fibers; a constant supersingular factor gives a finite exceptional locus, while outside that case Frobenius-graph isogenies yield many exceptional points.

proof_stages.label: Mixed-characteristic theorem and CM corollary
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem is stated: for any two elliptic curves over a number field there are infinitely many places at which their reductions become geometrically isogenous, and comparison with a CM elliptic curve gives a corollary on infinitely many supersingular or prescribed CM reductions of a single elliptic curve.

proof_stages.label: Frobenius-trace probability heuristic
proof_stages.summary: Over Q, isogeny of the reductions is equivalent to equality of the Frobenius traces, and independent Sato-Tate behavior in a Weil interval suggests probability about p^{-1/2}, whose divergent sum predicts infinitely many such primes and even a stronger rational-isogeny statement.

proof_stages.label: Hecke-correspondence reformulation on X(1)
proof_stages.summary: After replacing the elliptic curves by horizontal divisors y and z on the arithmetic surface X(1), the desired primes are encoded as points of z intersecting the Hecke translates T_N^*y, because such points are exactly cyclic N-isogenies between the reductions.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic height growth and local decomposition
proof_stages.summary: Using the metrized Hodge line bundle M=j^*O(1) with the Petersson metric and a known height formula for Hecke translates, the global height/intersection grows like deg(T_N) log N and decomposes into finite local intersection terms plus an Archimedean Green-function term.

proof_stages.label: Local equidistribution and worst-approximation bounds
proof_stages.summary: To force new primes, one must show that any fixed finite set of places contributes only o(deg(T_N) log N): multiplicative places are handled explicitly by Tate uniformization, good and Archimedean places by Hecke equidistribution for most points, and the remaining very-close approximations by a two-degree test forcing proximity to separated CM points.

proof_stages.label: Limitations of the mixed-characteristic geometry
proof_stages.summary: The speaker closes by pointing out that other mixed-characteristic Noether-Lefschetz phenomena, such as parity-forced Picard jumps for K3 reductions, are not visible through the present Hecke/intersection strategy.

examples.name: Constant supersingular elliptic-curve factor over a finite-field curve
examples.note: Taking one elliptic curve constant supersingular and the other nonconstant makes the product non-isotrivial but gives only finitely many fibers where the two are isogenous, showing that the naive finite-field analogue has a genuine exceptional case.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker constructs the bad case with one constant supersingular elliptic curve and one nonconstant curve, then explains why the Noether-Lefschetz/isogeny locus is finite via supersingular fibers.

passes.reason: The constant factor is moduli-collapsed while supersingularity is the p-rank-zero extreme, and it is explicitly the bad case where the density theorem fails.
A constant supersingular factor is isotrivial/low-variation and p-rank-zero extremal, and it is presented as the bad case where the finite-field density statement fails.

The constant supersingular factor is isotrivial with trivial variation and supersingular extremal Newton/endomorphism behavior, and it is explicitly the bad case making the finite-characteristic density statement fail.
supersingular is a Newton/p-rank extreme and the constant factor is isotrivial, and it is used as the bad case where the expected infinite Noether-Lefschetz/isogeny locus is finite.
A constant factor is an isotrivial/trivial family and supersingularity is the minimal-p-rank extreme, used as the explicit bad case where the isogeny/Noether–Lefschetz locus is finite.
the constant supersingular factor collapses variation and gives the specific bad case where the finite-field density statement fails.
It is the bad case making the finite-field density statement fail, with a constant/isotrivial factor and supersingular reduction at the extremal Newton-polygon/p-rank boundary.
It is the finite-field bad case making the density theorem false, with a constant/trivial factor and supersingularity a boundary/minimal-p-rank condition.
It is the bad finite-field case making the Noether-Lefschetz locus finite; the factor is constant/isotrivial and supersingular is the elliptic Newton-polygon extreme.
It is the explicit bad case where the finite-field analogue has finite Noether–Lefschetz locus, with constant variation degenerate and supersingularity extremal via minimal p-rank/Newton behavior.
It is the stated bad case making the Noether–Lefschetz locus finite, with a constant/isotrivial factor and supersingular elliptic curve at an extremal Newton stratum.
It is the bad case making the finite-field Noether–Lefschetz locus finite, with a constant zero-variation factor and supersingular minimal-p-rank behavior.

examples.name: Three elliptic curves over Q
examples.note: The speaker considers whether three elliptic curves should become mutually isogenous modulo infinitely many primes; it illustrates that the trace-counting heuristic still suggests infinitude even though the geometric Noether-Lefschetz analogy becomes dimensionally unclear.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker applies the trace-equality heuristic to three elliptic curves over Q, estimating the probability and noting that the resulting sum over primes still diverges.

passes.reason: An arbitrary triple of elliptic curves over Q is used as a typical heuristic/open test case, not to refute a claim.
A triple of elliptic curves over Q is used as an ordinary heuristic test case for simultaneous isogeny after reduction, not as a structurally special object or refutation.
A general triple of elliptic curves over Q is used as a speculative test case rather than as a structurally special object or a refutation.
an arbitrary triple of elliptic curves over Q is a typical higher-codimension test case, raised speculatively rather than used to refute a claim.
Three elliptic curves over Q are treated as a generic heuristic extension of the two-curve problem, not as a structurally special object or refutation.
the triple case is the marginal Frobenius-trace heuristic case with expected probability about 1/p, but it is posed as a speculative extension rather than a disproof.
The triple is raised as a speculative test case rather than a disproof, and it is the borderline case in the trace-equality heuristic where the expected sum is only just divergent.
A generic triple of elliptic curves over Q is raised as a speculative extension/test case rather than as a refutation.
A triple of elliptic curves over Q is raised as a heuristic/open extension, with no special intrinsic structure specified.
A triple of elliptic curves over Q is posed as a heuristic borderline question rather than a refutation, and is structurally an ordinary tuple.
It is posed as a generic speculative extension where the heuristic still predicts infinitude, not as a disproof or structurally special triple.
A triple of elliptic curves over Q is raised as a speculative test case for the heuristic rather than as a refutation, and is structurally ordinary.

examples.name: Picard-rank-one K3 reductions and parity
examples.note: A K3 surface over Q with Picard number one is mentioned as a contrasting situation where parity forces Picard-number jumps after reduction, illustrating a mixed-characteristic Noether-Lefschetz phenomenon not explained by the Hecke-intersection method.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker only cites the Picard-rank-one K3 reduction parity phenomenon as a contrasting case, stating that Picard number must jump without working through the example.

passes.reason: Picard rank one is the minimum possible for a projective K3, and the parity jump in reductions is cited as a distinct phenomenon rather than a counterexample.
A Picard-rank-one projective K3 has minimal Néron-Severi rank, while the parity jump is cited as a contrasting phenomenon rather than a counterexample to a stated claim.
A projective K3 surface of Picard rank one has the minimal possible Picard rank, and the parity-forced jump in reduction is cited as an unexplained phenomenon rather than a counterexample.
Picard rank one is the minimal projective K3 Picard rank, and the example is used as a parity-forced obstruction to the preceding geometric picture.
A projective K3 with Picard rank one is at the minimal Picard-rank extreme, and the parity-forced jump is cited as a phenomenon rather than a counterexample.
Picard rank one is the minimal projective K3 Picard rank, and parity is invoked as a separate phenomenon rather than a counterexample.
It illustrates a parity phenomenon not explained by the method, while Picard rank one is the minimal Picard rank for a projective K3 surface.
The K3 case is used to illustrate a parity phenomenon not captured by the method, and Picard rank one is the minimal projective K3 Picard rank.
It is cited as a parity-driven obstruction to the geometric method, and Picard rank one is the minimal possible rank for a projective K3 surface.
A Picard-rank-one K3 is used to illustrate a parity phenomenon in reductions, and rank one is the minimal Picard rank for a projective K3.
It is raised as an obstruction to the geometric method, since parity forces Picard jumps from the minimal Picard-rank-one K3 case after finite-field reduction.
A projective K3 surface with Picard rank one is at the minimal Picard-rank extreme and is used to illustrate parity-forced jumps rather than to disprove a claim.
